query: clinical studies on fixed-dose combination antiretrovirals and their impact on patient quality of life

retrieved_docs.title: Ritonavir -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the indications for ritonavir. Outline the potential adverse effects of ritonavir. Summarize the mechanism of action of ritonavir, both as an antiretroviral on its own and in its use with other agents. Outline the importance of collaboration and coordination among the interprofessional team to enhance the care of HIV patients when dosing with ritonavir to help limit side effects and improve patient outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Highly Active Antiretroviral Therapy (HAART) -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Reduce morbidity and mortality (AIDS and non-AIDS associated causes) Improve the quality of life Reduce plasma viral RNA load Prevent transmission to others (sex partners, needle-sharing partners, mother to infant) Prevent drug resistance Improve immune function

retrieved_docs.title: Abacavir -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify patients who require antiretroviral therapy, including abacavir, for managing HIV-1 based on clinical guidelines and diagnostic criteria. Implement and integrate abacavir into antiretroviral regimens efficiently, adhering to prescribed dosages and appropriate combinations as per established HIV treatment protocols. Assess patient responses to abacavir therapy regularly, monitoring treatment adherence, viral load suppression, and potential drug-related complications through laboratory tests and clinical evaluations. Determine strategies with other healthcare team members to ensure seamless implementation, monitoring, and optimization of abacavir therapy within the broader context of HIV management. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Abacavir -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Recommendations for Increasing Successful Outcomes in Antiretroviral Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Regimens of similar efficacy and tolerability, once-daily regimens are recommended in treatment-naïve patients starting ART and experienced patients receiving poorly tolerated/complex regimens. [26] [27] [28] [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Efavirenz -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify potential candidates for efavirenz-based HIV therapy based on clinical guidelines and patient profiles. Implement efavirenz therapy with appropriate dosing, emphasizing the importance of adherence and addressing concerns about adverse effects. Assess patients regularly for medication efficacy and any adverse reactions, focusing on viral load suppression and CD4 count improvement. Develop effective communication with patients about efavirenz therapy, addressing potential adverse effects, adherence, and expectations. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome Antiretroviral Therapy(Archived) -- Treatment / Management -- Who and how to treat?
retrieved_docs.content: HIV antiretroviral therapy should always be done with combination regimens. Initial treatment usually consists of two NRTIs (usually abacavir/lamivudine or tenofovir/emtricitabine) in combination with a third antiviral drug from one of the following classes: integrase inhibitor, NNRTI, or protease inhibitor plus an enhancer (cobicistat or ritonavir). This is based on the proven efficacy of this regimen and its more favorable side effect profile. Alternative treatments may be considered in special patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Efavirenz -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment with efavirenz can significantly improve the quality of life of patients infected with HIV, but its efficacy heavily relies on patient adherence. The neurologic adverse effects associated with efavirenz cause some patients to discontinue therapy. An interprofessional team of healthcare providers must become actively involved in caring for their HIV-positive patients. Key members of the team include pharmacists, physicians, advanced practice practitioners, nurses, and social workers. Obstacles to successful ART include a lack of social support and the financial burden of therapy. Healthcare providers must provide counsel regarding methods of coping with these adverse effects and promote medication adherence. Identifying appropriate opportunities for members of each discipline to provide care and encouragement is crucial to successful ART. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the mechanism of action of HIV medications approved by the Food and Drug administration. Identify the adverse effects of HIV antiretroviral therapy medications. Determine the appropriate monitoring steps of antiretroviral therapy medications. Communicate the importance of improving care coordination amongst the interprofessional team to enhance care delivery for patients with HIV and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Highly Active Antiretroviral Therapy (HAART) -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Many of these medications are administered orally, once a day, in a co-formulated combination tablet. However, some medications are not yet in combination formulas, thus creating a large pill burden for patients who require a regimen of three or four drugs twice daily. The increased number of pills can decrease patient adherence, particularly for patients in resource-limited settings, patients with dysphagia, or patients who struggle with the financial burden of a HAART regimen. For patients who have dysphagia or trouble swallowing for any reason, there are liquid preparations or crushable tablets, though dosages may need to be adjusted. The Food and Drug Administration has recently been approving medications containing two or three-drug, a fixed-dose regimen in a single tablet. Combination medications with once-daily dosing are preferred as these regimens have been shown to increase patient compliance up to three times over more complex dosing regimens. [9] [29] [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Emtricitabine -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Every patient should receive an HIV test at least once in their lives, with specific populations needing more frequent testing. Men having sex with men, transgender women, and intravenous drug users should have HIV testing at least one time per year and as frequently as every three months. Patients who present with sexually transmitted infections and HCV should be screened for HIV more often. [4] [Level 5 [4] When testing for HIV, the health care team needs to explain how HIV testing methodology, how long until the results return, and that they must report positive results to the state health department. [17] HIV testing recommendations include combination antibody-antigen assay or combination antibody assay with nucleic acid testing. [4] [Level 3] Further testing of the HIV viral load is the recommendation before prescribing ART. [4] [Level 1] Patients found to be HIV-positive need a referral to infectious disease or an HIV specialist. Further testing of HIV viral load and HIV genotype should take place before prescribing ART. HIV genotype testing before medical therapy shows which medications the specific strain of HIV may have resistance. [4] [Level 5] The comorbidities of these patients need to be documented well in the electronic health record, especially if the patient has concurrent HBV. Liver enzymes and HBV DNA viral load become elevated when a patient stops taking emtricitabine abruptly. [1] Primary care and infectious disease providers need to monitor the patient for signs of liver damage for weeks after discontinuing emtricitabine. [1] It is also essential for a health care provider to address the mental health of a newly diagnosed HIV patient and those living with HIV, with referral to psychiatry or counseling a possibility. The health care team must keep track of when their patients receive their medications, and when they come to their appointments. [4] [Level 3] [4] The entire office, including the front desk administration, can assist with reminding patients of appointments and follow-ups. [4] [Level 1] Every visit presents an opportunity to discuss adherence to ART, screen for an understanding of medications, and reiterate the importance of safe sex practices and honesty of diagnosis for those with HIV. Screening the social factors of this patient population is vital to guarantee these patients can afford their medications, understand how to take their medications, have support from family and friends, and have access to proper transportation to and from the physician’s office. It may be necessary to bring in a social worker to help further address the social needs of this patient population.

retrieved_docs.title: Indinavir (Archived) -- Adverse Effects -- Most Common Less Serve Side Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Diarrhea Nausea Vomiting Fatigue

retrieved_docs.title: Didanosine (Archived) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The World Health Organization (WHO), 2015 guidelines recommended the initiation of antiretroviral therapy in all HIV-positive patients. Studies have shown that early administration of antiretroviral therapy significantly decreased cost as well as adverse patient outcomes. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Highly Active Antiretroviral Therapy (HAART) -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the treatment considerations for patients with HIV. Identify the most common adverse events associated with highly active antiretroviral therapy (HAART) medications. Summarize the significant monitoring parameters in patients on highly active antiretroviral therapy (HAART) therapy. Outline the importance of collaboration and communication amongst the interprofessional team to ensure appropriate selection of Highly active antiretroviral therapy (HAART) regimens to improve HIV- positive patient outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Lamivudine -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the specific indications for lamivudine in managing HIV-1 and hepatitis B infections, understanding its role within antiretroviral therapy. Screen patients to assess their suitability for lamivudine therapy, considering factors such as viral load, resistance patterns, and liver function tests. Assess the patient's response to lamivudine therapy, monitor viral load, liver function, and potential adverse effects, and adjust treatment based on these assessments. Implement communication with patients about the benefits, potential risks, and expected outcomes of lamivudine therapy, facilitating shared decision-making and promoting adherence. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.content: Daily adherence Long-term medication toxicity Cost of treatment Limited options for those with multiclass resistance [26]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Prevention -- Clinical Significance -- Preexposure Prophylaxis
retrieved_docs.content: The choice of PrEP regime should be based on a discussion between the person and provider and based on the person's risk factor profile, comorbid conditions, preference for oral or injectable medications, and ability to take medications reliably, in addition to the adverse effect profile and cost of the medication if not covered through insurance or pharmaceutical-sponsored programs. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 73
retrieved_docs.content: Direct‑acting antivirals (DAAs) encompass several pharmacologic classes used to treat viral infections, including HIV; within DAAs, cyclic amines such as rimantadin alter cellular synthetic activity, while integrase inhibitors, non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) and nucleoside/nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) constitute additional subclasses. NRTIs include abacavir (synonymous with dideoxyadenosine), dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine, and tenofovir disoproxil, and are employed both for HIV prevention (e.g., abacavir, emtricitabine, lamivudine) and therapy. NNRTIs comprise delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesylate, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, doravirine, rilpivirine, and raltegravir‑type agents that bind distinct sites on reverse transcriptase; doravirine is contraindicated with rifampicin and rilpivirine with pantoprazole due to drug‑drug interaction risks. Integrase inhibitors—dolutegravir, elvitegravir, raltegravir, cabotegravir, and fostemsavir—block the viral integrase enzyme required for proviral integration. Fixed‑dose combination regimens such as abacavir/dolutegravir/lamivudine and abacavir/lamivudine (also representing dideoxyadenosine/lamivudine) are classified as “Antivirals for treatment of HIV infections, combinations” and exemplify DAA‑based HIV therapy. Antiviral combinations specifically formulated for HIV are themselves a type of DAA. Shared needle use is identified as a direct risk factor for HIV acquisition, whereas methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in patients with HIV infection, underscoring important safety considerations in this population. Together, these entities delineate the classification, mechanisms of action, therapeutic and preventive roles, combination formulations, nomenclature equivalences, and contraindications relevant to comprehensive HIV management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 706
retrieved_docs.content: HIV is a chronic viral infection that can be managed with antiretroviral therapy, and tenofovir disoproxil is an antiretroviral prodrug that may be used to treat HIV infection. Tenofovir disoproxil works by inhibiting viral replication, thereby helping to suppress HIV viral load, preserve immune function, and reduce disease progression, making it a key component of comprehensive HIV patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 0 -- Community 7
retrieved_docs.content: Direct‑acting antivirals constitute a broad pharmacologic group that is organized by therapeutic indication, mechanism of action, and anatomical site of use. Within the hepatitis C subclass, ribavirin and the oligopeptide‑derived telaprevir illustrate the use of peptide scaffolds in antiviral design. The HIV‑combination subclass includes fixed‑dose regimens abacavir / dolutegravir / lamivudine and abacavir / lamivudine, both belonging to the larger HIV‑combination antiviral group. Additional antiretrovirals for HIV infection comprise the entry inhibitor enfuvirtide, the fusion‑inhibitor ibalizumab, nucleoside and nucleotide reverse‑transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) such as abacavir, tenofovir disoproxil, lamivudine, emtricitabine, stavudine, zidovudine, and dideoxycytidine, non‑nucleoside reverse‑transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) including delavirdine (and delavirdine‑mesylate), efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, and rilpivirine (the latter contraindicated with pantoprazole), and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1993
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors are a class of direct‑acting antiviral agents that target viral protease enzymes essential for the replication of certain viruses, most notably HIV. Key members of this class include fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir, each of which is classified as a protease inhibitor. By inhibiting the protease step in the viral life cycle, these drugs prevent the maturation of infectious viral particles and are integral components of combination antiretroviral therapy used to achieve viral suppression in patients with HIV infection. Understanding that fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir all belong to the protease inhibitor group helps clinicians select appropriate regimens, anticipate class‑related effects, and manage drug‑drug interactions within comprehensive HIV care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1732
retrieved_docs.content: Fosamprenavir, amprenavir, and tipranavir are all classified as protease inhibitors that belong to the group of direct‑acting antiviral agents. As protease inhibitors, each drug specifically targets viral protease enzymes, a critical step in the replication cycle of viruses such as HIV, thereby providing a mechanism of action that directly interferes with viral maturation. Their shared categorization as direct‑acting antivirals highlights that they act on viral components rather than host cellular processes, which informs clinicians about common therapeutic objectives, potential for cross‑resistance, and similar considerations regarding drug‑drug interactions and adverse effect profiles in antiretroviral treatment regimens.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2629
retrieved_docs.content: Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors are a class of antiretroviral agents that act by binding to HIV reverse transcriptase and inhibiting viral replication. The drugs efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapin, and rilpivirine are all members of the non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor class. Recognizing that these agents belong to the same pharmacologic group helps clinicians understand their shared mechanism of action, anticipate class‑related effects, and make informed choices when selecting or switching antiretroviral therapy for patients living with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2156
retrieved_docs.content: Dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir are all members of the class of integrase inhibitors that are used as systemic anti‑infective agents. As integrase inhibitors, these drugs target the HIV integrase enzyme, preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and thereby forming a key component of antiretroviral therapy for HIV infection. Recognizing that each of these agents belongs to the same pharmacologic class helps clinicians select appropriate regimens, understand class‑related efficacy and safety profiles, and manage drug interactions in the care of patients living with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 135
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is strongly associated with shared needle use, which constitutes a major transmission route, and its prevention and treatment rely on antiretroviral agents classified by mechanism of action. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, mimic natural nucleosides, and cause chain termination, thereby inhibiting viral DNA synthesis. Key NRTIs include tenofovir disoproxil, abacavir, didanosine (dideoxycytidine), emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, and zidovudine; each has demonstrated activity in pre‑exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) and in early or combination treatment regimens to suppress viral replication. The non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor delavirdine mesylate also possesses preventive properties by directly inhibiting reverse transcriptase. Integrase inhibitors—cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir—block the viral integrase enzyme, preventing integration of viral DNA into the host genome; cabotegravir is notable for its dual use in both treatment of established infection and PrEP, while the other agents are primarily employed for treatment but may be used preventively in high‑risk settings. Collectively, the correct classification and combined use of NRTIs, non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors, and integrase inhibitors form the backbone of modern HIV prevention strategies and therapeutic protocols, optimizing patient outcomes through viral suppression and reduced acquisition risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 0 -- Community 18
retrieved_docs.content: is an NRTI; and abacavir, dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine and zidovudine constitute the NRTI class. Abacavir belongs to the dideoxyadenosine subclass and is incorporated into fixed‑dose combinations with dolutegravir and lamivudine as well as a two‑drug regimen with lamivudine. Drug‑drug safety considerations include a contraindicated interaction between doravirine and rifapentine and between pantoprazole and rilpivirine. HIV infection is linked to the broader category of viral infection, to the transmission risk factor of shared needle use, and may limit the effectiveness or use of raltegravir, rilpivirine, stavudine and zidovudine. Peptide‑derived compounds such as acetylphenylalanyl‑prolyl‑boroarginine, deltorphin I, Ala(2)‑ and the protease inhibitor telaprevir are mapped from an oligopeptidyl scaffold, indicating a common biochemical origin. Additional antiviral agents include nucleosides/nucleotides that are not RT inhibitors (brincidofovir, the

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1685
retrieved_docs.content: Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) are a class of antiretroviral drugs that inhibit the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme by binding to a site distinct from the active site, thereby preventing viral replication. The NNRTI class includes the agents delavirdin, etravirine, and nevirapin, each of which shares this mechanism of action while differing in pharmacokinetic properties, resistance profiles, and clinical use. Delavirdin was one of the earlier NNRTIs and is administered three times daily, whereas etravirine is a newer, more potent NNRTI often used in treatment‑experienced patients due to its activity against certain resistant HIV strains. Nevirapin (commonly known as nevirapine) is widely used in both adult and pediatric regimens and is notable for its long half‑life and potential for hypersensitivity reactions. Recognizing that delavirdin, etravirine, and nevirapin all belong to the Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor class helps clinicians select appropriate therapy, anticipate cross‑resistance, and manage side‑effect profiles in the comprehensive care of individuals living with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 0 -- Community 5
retrieved_docs.content: inhibitor maraviroc, the attachment inhibitor fostemsavir, the fusion inhibitor enfuvirtide, protease‑targeting agents, and the monoclonal antibody ibalizumab. Fixed‑dose HIV combinations such as abacavir/dolutegravir/lamivudine and abacavir/lamivudine are classified within this therapeutic class. The ophthalmologic antiviral subgroup contains famciclovir, ganciclovir, trifluridine and vidarabine; ganciclovir, vidarabine and cidofovir belong to the “nucleosides and nucleotides excl. reverse transcriptase inhibitors” class and act as nucleic‑acid‑synthesis inhibitors, while rimantadine is a cyclic‑amine DAA that alters cellular synthetic activity. Fludarabine is synonymous with vidarabine. A separate pharmacologic class of nucleosides and nucleotides that do not inhibit reverse transcriptase includes brincidofovir, brivudine (chemically related to bromodeoxyuridine) and remdesivir; remdesivir is indicated for viral infections and ribavirin may be used for prophylaxis. Brincidofovir and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1405
retrieved_docs.content: Delavirdin, efavirenz, and etravirine are all classified as non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs). As NNRTIs, these agents bind to a distinct site on the HIV‑1 reverse transcriptase enzyme, inducing conformational changes that inhibit viral DNA synthesis. By sharing this mechanism of action, delavirdin, efavirenz, and etravirine are employed in antiretroviral therapy regimens for HIV infection, often combined with nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors and other drug classes to achieve maximal viral suppression and reduce the risk of resistance. Their common membership in the NNRTI class highlights their pharmacologic similarity and guides clinicians in selecting appropriate regimens based on efficacy, side‑effect profiles, and resistance patterns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 90
retrieved_docs.content: Direct acting antivirals (DAAs) encompass several pharmacologic classes used in the management of HIV infection. The class “Antivirals for treatment of HIV infections, combinations” includes fixed‑dose regimens such as abacavir/dolutegravir/lamivudine and abacavir/lamivudine. DAA subclasses comprise cyclic amines (e.g., rimantadine, which alters cellular synthetic activity), integrase inhibitors (cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir, raltegravir), non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) (delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesylate, rilpivirine, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine) and nucleoside/nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) (tenofovir disoproxil, abacavir, dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine, dideoxyadenosine). These agents can be employed for pre‑exposure prophylaxis or for achieving viral suppression in infected patients. Specific agents noted for prophylactic or therapeutic use include abacavir, delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesylate, dolutegravir, efavirenz, elvitegravir, emtricitabine, etravirine and lamivudine, while cabotegravir is indicated for treatment of HIV infection. Drug‑drug incompatibilities include the contraindication of methotrexate sodium in patients with HIV infection and the interaction requiring avoidance of pantoprazole with rilpivirine. Shared needle use (“společné užívání jehel”) is identified as a transmission risk factor for HIV. Understanding the classification, mechanisms, combination products, cross‑resistance potential and contraindications of these antiviral agents is essential for optimal patient care and therapeutic decision‑making in HIV management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1216
retrieved_docs.content: Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors constitute a class of antiretroviral agents that target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, thereby blocking viral replication. Tenofovir disoproxil, abacavir, dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, and stavudine are all members of this nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitor class. By incorporating these drugs into combination antiretroviral therapy, clinicians can achieve more effective suppression of HIV, improve patient outcomes, and reduce the risk of resistance development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 105
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is primarily transmitted through shared needle use, and its prevention and treatment rely on a broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents that target the viral reverse transcriptase enzyme. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) – including tenofovir disoproxil, emtricitabine (also referenced as emtricitabin), lamivudine (lamivudin), stavudine (stavudin), zidovudine (zidovudin), and dideoxycytidine – act as nucleoside analogues that are incorporated into viral DNA and cause premature chain termination, forming a core component of combination HIV therapy. Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) – doravirine, delavirdine (and its mesylate formulation), efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, and rilpivirine – bind to a distinct site on reverse transcriptase to inhibit viral replication. Doravirine has a specific contraindication when co‑administered with the rifamycin antibiotic rifapentine, reflecting a clinically important drug‑class interaction. Understanding the class membership, mechanisms of action, preventive potential, and contraindications of these NRTI and NNRTI agents is essential for optimizing patient care, selecting effective regimens, managing drug interactions, and reducing the spread of HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1227
retrieved_docs.content: Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors constitute a class of antiretroviral drugs that target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, thereby blocking viral replication. This therapeutic class includes tenofovir disoproxil, emtricitabine, lamivudine, zidovudine, and dideoxycytidine (also known as zalcitabine). By sharing a common mechanism of action, these agents are frequently combined in HIV treatment regimens to achieve synergistic viral suppression and reduce the risk of resistance development.
